--- title: "AB Testing is an Optimization Problem" subtitle: "Why Traditional Stopping Rules Are Arbitrary" author: "Russ Zaliznyak" date: "2026-05-22" categories: [AB Testing, Bayesian, Decision Theory, Optimization] page-layout: full jupyter: py311 execute: echo: false css: wider.css --- ::: {.post-meta} [In Progress]{.badge-wip} ::: Ronald Fisher chose p < 0.05 in 1925 to analyze crop yields. A century later, it's still the default for product experimentation — not because it's optimal, but because it was never questioned. Fisher himself noted: > *"The calculation is absurdly academic, for in fact no scientific worker has a fixed level > of significance at which from year to year, and in all circumstances, he rejects hypotheses."* > > — Ronald Fisher, *Statistical Methods and Scientific Inference* (1956) # What Drives Stopping Criteria? The stopping rule that minimizes regret isn't universal — it depends on the economics of your specific test. Four inputs determine it: ```{=html}
The pre-experiment conversion rate. Sets the variance of your test statistic and determines how much room for improvement exists.
Daily users in the experiment and their control/treatment split. More traffic means faster evidence. Uneven splits trade speed for risk protection.
How long the winning variant runs after the decision. A longer deployment horizon means more value at stake — justifying a more aggressive stopping rule.
The realistic lift you expect. Larger effects are detectable faster. Smaller effects require more traffic or more tolerance for uncertainty.
``` ```{=html}
